title: Stay Protected This Fall: Your Essential Guide to COVID-19, Flu, and RSV Vaccines
summary: As we approach respiratory virus season, Syra Madad writes an essential guide to stay safe.
body: As we approach respiratory virus season, staying up-to-date with vaccinations for COVID-19, flu, and RSV is essential for keeping yourself and your loved ones safe. Here’s what you need to know about this year’s vaccines. COVID-19 Vaccines: What’s New? This season brings updated vaccines tailored to the latest COVID-19 variants: Novavax's Updated COVID-19 Vaccine Approved for individuals aged 12 and older, this monovalent, protein-based vaccine targets the JN.1 Omicron subvariant, which was predominant last winter. It provides an alternative to the mRNA vaccines from Moderna and Pfizer for those seeking protection this season. Updated Pfizer and Moderna Vaccines These vaccines are designed to target the KP.2 variant—an offshoot of JN.1—and its related strains, KP.3.1.1 and KP.2.3, which currently account for over 64% of circulating variants. The updated vaccines are recommended for everyone aged 6 months and older. Emerging Variants The newest subvariant, XEC, part of the Omicron family, combines lesser-known subvariants KS.1.1 and KP.3.3. Although detected in the U.S., XEC currently represents a small portion of cases and is not yet tracked by the CDC. Free COVID-19 Tests The U.S. Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) has relaunched its free COVID-19 testing program. Testing remains vital for knowing if you’re infected and preventing spread to others. Visit covidtests.gov to order four free at-home COVID-19 tests. Why Get the Updated COVID-19 Vaccine? Everyone aged 6 months and older should receive the 2024-2025 COVID-19 vaccine, which offers the best defense against severe illness, hospitalization, and death. Updated vaccines are tailored to the most recent strains, providing enhanced protection, even for those previously vaccinated. It’s especially important for: Individuals who have never received a COVID-19 vaccine. Those aged 65 and older. People at high risk for severe COVID-19, including those with underlying health conditions. Residents of long-term care facilities. Pregnant or breastfeeding individuals, and those planning to become pregnant. Anyone looking to reduce their risk of Long COVID. Flu Vaccine: What’s Different This Year? This year’s flu vaccine targets three strains: two influenza A viruses (H1N1 and H3N2) and one influenza B/Victoria virus. Notably, it marks a return to trivalent formulations, excluding the Influenza B/Yamagata component present in the previous quadrivalent vaccines. Why Get the Flu Vaccine? The flu vaccine prevents millions of illnesses and flu-related medical visits each year. During the 2019-2020 flu season, vaccination prevented an estimated 7 million cases, 3 million doctor visits, 100,000 hospitalizations, and 7,000 deaths. Benefits include reducing your chance of infection, minimizing the severity and duration of illness if infected, and lowering the risk of hospitalization and death. RSV Vaccine: Protecting Older Adults and Pregnant Individuals The CDC recommends the RSV vaccine for: Adults Aged 75 and Older: Vaccination is recommended for everyone in this age group. Adults Aged 60-74 at Increased Risk: This includes those with chronic heart or lung disease, other chronic conditions, and residents of long-term care facilities. Maternal RSV Vaccination: Pregnant individuals between 32 to 36 weeks are advised to receive the RSV vaccine to help protect newborns during their vulnerable early months. When should you get the RSV Vaccine? The ideal time for the RSV vaccine is in late summer or early fall, just before the virus starts spreading. It is not an annual vaccine, so those who have already received it do not need another dose this season. Final Thoughts: Protect Yourself and Your Community Vaccination against COVID-19, flu, and RSV is the best way to protect yourself and those around you. Alongside vaccination, wearing a well-fitting mask, practicing good respiratory and hand hygiene, can further reduce your risk of illness. Stay protected, stay healthy, and enjoy the season with peace of mind. result.main_content_en: The article provides a comprehensive guide to the 2024-2025 respiratory virus season vaccinations. For COVID-19, updated vaccines are available: Novavax's protein-based vaccine targets the JN.1 variant, while updated Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines target the KP.2 variant and its related strains. The CDC recommends the updated COVID-19 vaccine for everyone aged 6 months and older, especially for high-risk groups. The U.S. government is also offering free at-home COVID-19 tests. This year's flu vaccine is a trivalent formulation targeting two influenza A strains (H1N1, H3N2) and one influenza B strain, differing from last year's quadrivalent version. The RSV vaccine is recommended for adults aged 75 and older, adults aged 60-74 with certain risk factors, and pregnant individuals between 32 to 36 weeks gestation to protect newborns. The article emphasizes vaccination as the primary defense, supplemented by masks and hygiene, to reduce severe illness, hospitalization, and community spread.
result.main_content_zh: 本文是关于2024-2025年呼吸道病毒季节疫苗接种的指南。针对新冠病毒，本季有更新的疫苗：诺瓦瓦克斯的蛋白疫苗针对JN.1变体，而更新的辉瑞和莫德纳mRNA疫苗则针对KP.2变体及其相关毒株。美国疾控中心建议所有6个月及以上人群接种更新的新冠疫苗，特别是高风险群体。美国政府同时重启了免费家庭新冠检测计划。本季流感疫苗为三价，针对两种甲型流感病毒（H1N1和H3N2）和一种乙型流感病毒，与上季的四价疫苗不同。呼吸道合胞病毒（RSV）疫苗建议75岁及以上所有老年人、60-74岁有特定风险因素的成年人（如患有慢性心肺疾病）以及孕32至36周的孕妇接种，以保护新生儿。文章强调接种疫苗是预防重症、住院和社区传播的主要手段，辅以佩戴口罩和良好卫生习惯，可进一步降低患病风险。
